name: Sams Canyon
short_description: BLM rockhounding site in the Wasatch Range featuring quartz, chalcedony, and agate deposits in scenic canyon wash terrain. Free public collecting with no permit required.
description:
Sams Canyon is a scenic BLM rockhounding location in Wasatch County, Utah, offering excellent opportunities to find quartz, chalcedony, agate, and jasper specimens in a classic canyon wash setting. The site sits within the Wasatch Range, a region rich in mineral-bearing geologic formations created during the Laramide Orogeny and subsequent Basin and Range tectonics. The canyon wash environment naturally concentrates colorful specimens, making this an ideal location for both beginners and experienced collectors.

The terrain features a scenic canyon setting where geological uplift has exposed quartz-rich bedrock and brought down loose specimens from higher elevations. Walking the wash and surrounding benches typically yields agate nodules in various colors, chalcedony pieces, and quartz specimens. The area is relatively accessible compared to high-elevation Utah rockhounding sites, making it popular with families and those seeking an easier collecting experience.

Personal use collecting is allowed on BLM land under FLPMA regulations. The 25 lb per day limit is generous for typical rockhounders, allowing you to pocket several good specimens. Early morning visits often yield the best finds before other collectors have passed through, and seasonal runoff can expose fresh material in the wash. The canyon provides natural shade during hotter months, making spring and fall particularly pleasant for extended collecting sessions.
history: Sams Canyon sits within the Wasatch Range, formed during the Laramide Orogeny (70-40 million years ago) when tectonic compression uplifted metamorphic and igneous rocks. Subsequent Basin and Range extension and weathering have exposed these quartz-rich formations and created the canyon wash where specimens accumulate today, making this area a natural concentration zone for silica-based minerals.
tips: Search the wash floor and low benches first - gravity concentrates heavier agate and jasper nodules here over time. Look for specimens showing translucent orange, red, brown, or white chalcedony and banded agate patterns; these stand out against gray country rock. Bring a 5-gallon bucket to haul finds and stay hydrated - canyon shade helps but the terrain is exposed in places. Late spring after snowmelt refreshes the wash with newly exposed material.
terrain_notes: Sandy and gravelly canyon wash with scattered boulders and rocky benches. Elevation approximately 5,500-6,000 feet with scattered sage and juniper. Walking is generally easy on wash floor but benches have uneven footing requiring sturdy boots. Access typically involves 0.5-2 miles of walking from parking depending on which section you collect.
written_directions: From Park City, take Utah State Route 224 northeast toward Kamas for approximately 8 miles. Continue on SR 224 as it becomes a county road heading toward Samak. Look for Sams Canyon turnoff on the right (east) side - it may be marked or unmarked depending on BLM signage. Follow the dirt road for approximately 1-2 miles until you reach a parking pullout near the canyon wash. From Kamas, Sams Canyon is approximately 12-15 miles north on local roads.
road_conditions: Dirt and gravel county road with moderate washboard, passable for high-clearance passenger vehicles in dry conditions. May require 4WD after wet weather or early season snowmelt.
parking_notes: Small dirt pullout near canyon entrance with space for 3-5 vehicles. No formal parking lot or facilities. High-clearance recommended for the final approach road.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. No digging in solid rock or bedrock without express BLM permission. Collect only loose specimens from wash and surface.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No digging in bedrock
Loose specimens only
nearest_services: Park City, Utah - 10-12 miles south with restaurants, lodging, fuel, and supplies. Kamas - 12-15 miles south with basic services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively easy terrain suitable for most visitors. Canyon wash floor is sandy and level. Uneven rocky benches require sturdy hiking boots. No wheelchair access to collecting areas. Minimal elevation gain from parking to productive collecting zones. Suitable for families with children aged 6 and up in reasonable hiking condition.

faq.question: What gemstones can you find rockhounding at Sams Canyon near Park City, Utah?
faq.answer: Sams Canyon is known for quartz, chalcedony, agate, and jasper specimens. Agate is the primary collector target, found in nodules displaying orange, red, brown, white, and gray colors. Chalcedony pieces are abundant and often translucent. Jasper and quartz chips are common in the wash.

faq.question: Is a permit required for rockhounding at Sams Canyon BLM land in Utah?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use rockhounding at Sams Canyon. It is located on public BLM land in Utah where recreational mineral collecting is allowed under FLPMA regulations. You may collect up to 25 lbs per day for personal use at no cost.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Sams Canyon?
faq.answer: April through October is ideal. Spring (April-May) is excellent because snowmelt refreshes the wash with newly exposed specimens. Fall (September-October) offers cooler temperatures and lower crowds. Summer heat is significant at this elevation but the canyon provides some shade. Winter access can be difficult due to snow.

faq.question: How far is Sams Canyon rockhounding site from Park City, and what roads do you take?
faq.answer: Sams Canyon is approximately 11 miles northeast of Park City. Take Utah State Route 224 northeast toward Kamas for about 8 miles, then follow county roads to the canyon turnoff. The final 1-2 miles is a rough dirt road requiring high-clearance vehicles. Total drive time is 20-30 minutes from downtown Park City.

faq.question: Is Sams Canyon rockhounding suitable for beginners and families with children?
faq.answer: Yes, Sams Canyon is beginner and family-friendly. The canyon wash terrain is relatively easy to walk, and loose specimens are plentiful on the surface without need for digging tools. Children age 6 and up can enjoy collecting. The 25 lb daily limit is generous, and no special skills are required to find quality agate and quartz.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should you bring for rockhounding at Sams Canyon?
faq.answer: Bring a 5-gallon bucket or backpack for specimens, sturdy hiking boots for rocky terrain, a small hand magnifier to identify specimens, and plenty of water (at least 2 liters). A field guide to Utah gemstones helps identify finds. Sunscreen and a hat are essential. No digging tools are needed since loose specimens are abundant in the wash.

meta_description: Find agate, quartz, and chalcedony at Sams Canyon near Park City, Utah. Free BLM rockhounding, no permit required. Easy terrain, family-friendly collecting.
